speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is September 18, 1996. The survivor is her Helena Horowitz, the last name at birth, Epstein. The interviewer is Rita Davis. We are in Teaneck, New Jersey, in the United States. The language of the interview is English. My name is Rita Davis, R-I-T-A D-A-V-I-S. Today's date is September 18, 1996. I am conducting an interview with Helena Horowitz. The interview is taking place in Teaneck, New Jersey, in the United States. Please give your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Helena Horowitz, H-E-L-E-N-A H-O-R-O-W-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth and spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Epstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-P-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any other names that you were known by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. During the war, I was known by the name of Gulia Sypek, G-U-L-I-A S-Y-P-E-K, or in Polish, [? Sypkuvna, ?] could be.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city and country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Pilzno, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-I-L-Z-N-O, Poland, P-O-L-A-N-D .

speaker: interviewer
text: What large city is Pilzno near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pilzno is 100 kilometers from Krakow, around 100 kilometers, K-R-A-K-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your family, the family into which you were born and the memories that you have of that family unit.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't remember too many things from my-- I remember my early childhood. I remember myself in the crib, really, vomiting or something. But my mother died when I was six. And I wasn't too much with her, you know, with my parents, because they-- they started the business. And I think these were difficult times for them. And I spend a lot of time with my grandparents. My parents lived in Debica. This was, I think, 12 kilometers from my birthplace and where my grandparents lived, and my father's brothers and sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far-- you said Debica was near your birth place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large a city or town was Debica?

speaker: interviewee
text: Debica was maybe 15,000, 20,000. I don't know, maybe less, have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Miriam, M-I-R-I-A-M Fredericks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know-- did you know Miriam, your mother's parents, your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me something about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived in another small town. They were-- it was-- they lived this small town, provincial life. They were well-off. They had a big house with warehouses and lumbers and some things. So she-- she grew up in this environment, very small town. There were no high schools there. But she-- shall I tell about her, or her background, or a little bit?

speaker: interviewer
text: Something about her background.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something about her background. And so she comes from this family. And she was-- I don't know anything about her childhood or about her-- her early years, only from my cousin here. Her cousin told me. And from what I found later, she was an intellectual. She was self-taught. They had, you know, education was very important. I know that she studied because in her letters to her brother here, she wrote that she is studying Hebrew. And she wrote to him. Her brother was in the United States. He immigrated 1917. And she wrote to him in German, this in English at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your actual memory of your mother is very limited.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's limited. I remember her in the kitchen. And I remember her, you know, I don't know what it was. Maybe she wasn't well. She wasn't very affectionate.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the cause--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of her death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Childbirth. She died in childbirth. She had a miscarriage. And she died at 32.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only child that she bore?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had a brother, a younger brother. And I don't know. Shall I say it? Maybe she preferred the brother than me. I don't know. I had this impression.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was a very hardworking businessman. I think in the beginning they struggled. I remember we had a little house. And I remember my mother. And we always had some help home. My father was working. There wasn't too much time. He did spend a lot of time the weekend, the Shabbat. Nobody worked. Then we were-- he was taking us for walks. I remember now that he was even, me and my brother, we had to ex-calisthenics he was doing with us. I remember this. You were there, and we had to follow. And I was going to kindergarten. It was like nursery here. And this was a town where nobody was afraid of-- of kidnapping or things like this. I was-- I-- maybe I was independent early, because I must have, you know, and I was probably five years old. My brother was younger. I was holding him by the hand. And I walked to the-- to school, to the nursery. I walked him. He was a-- my charge. And it was far away. But, you know, nobody was-- you know, you-- there were no buses. You just walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your father's parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: His father's parents, too, they lived there, very-- it was a very warm, loving family. They were very close to each other. Again, they had the store. They had a house. They lived behind the store. And the whole family was working. There was no help in-- at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business did they have?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had like a general store. And, again, a paints, and things like this. It was somehow runs the color, runs in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I mean, my grandfather, and my uncles, they were very talented. My grandfather, you know, he was wearing a beard. He was a religious, you know? Everybody was. There was no other-- other life. And I was told that there was this synagogue, right? And there is this arc where the Torahs are displayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he painted. I remember these lions painted, you know, holding up the thing. So he was the painter of, you know. And his sons, you know, they were-- they had facilities for throwing. I guess this is my-- it's hereditary. Yes, we can see that. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have mentioned that your grandfather and your father were somewhat religious. Were they Orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, traditional. You know, it was a small town. Everybody knew each other, just like probably here in the West with the Mormons or with the something. You had to conform. It was probably, you know, had to go to the synagogue. And the women were going all dressed up. You know, my town was rather progressive. People, it was important to be well-dressed. And I know my mother was a very-- she-- I don't remember her being talented. I don't know. It was my father that was helping me. But she was very intellectual. They, at the time, this was Austria before the Second World War, when before 1918. This was annexed by Austria, Galicia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had a-- they were bilingual, right, written, you know, German and Polish. And I found books, you know, in German, you know, philosophy or other books that she was such a self-taught. And maybe I always wanted to emulate her. I think so, maybe, maybe. What language was spoken in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish and Yiddish. But I didn't speak. I understand. But I didn't speak Yiddish. So maybe that's my Polish was a very good Polish. And I think this helped me in my survival, because nobody could detect any-- what say? I don't know how to explain a Brooklyn accent, right? And but my-- a lot of Yiddish was spoken in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with-- to you and your brother after your mother died?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember vividly somehow an incident. I don't know if I should talk about it because I don't know why it's so vivid. I was playing with my brother, you know, it was little house and we had a-- a playing outside of the house. And everything was very safe. You-- we could be outside. The doors were not closed. And I was playing with him, and we were running, you know, running after him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he fell on a stone. And he opened his-- he was wounded. He had a big open wound, you know? And I remember my mother-- that's very vivid-- running out of the house and screaming. And she just picked him up. And the blood was running from his wound in the forehead, and running with him to the doctor, you know? It was in the neighborhood. And maybe I thought that it was my fault. I don't know. And how did your family change after your mother's death?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, I didn't realize was pregnant at the time. And probably in June or something, end of it, she took me. I remember taking me to the-- to the public school and to the principal, right, a woman. And she registered me to-- to first grade. But I-- and then was-- this was before the vacation. And then I was taken away. I don't know, to my-- her parents. I was there for the summer. We were going a lot to grandparents for vacations or holidays. And, and so I was-- I was there on vacation. And I was not aware of anything wrong in the house. Nobody was saying. They didn't communicate with children much, you know, like it was in the old, you know, England, whatever. And then I remember one think was Friday night. And although this what the Shabbat, my-- we were sitting in the kitchen and my grandfather came in through the door. And he said something in Yiddish that I didn't remember. And my grandmother started screaming that-- her mother-- and hitting her head against the wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't know. I knew that something terrible had happened. But I wasn't told about this. So she died. And she died in the hospital in childbirth. She got, I think, blood poisoning. I don't know. They had no antibiotics, you know? But she was in the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, you know, the funny thing that they didn't-- nobody explained to me. So I was there. And this is-- I stayed already there with my grandparents. Nobody said anything. There was another cousin. And they were coming to me. And then I saw that they-- they were walking without their shoes, only they were sitting on the something, coming around. And it was this mood. And nobody ever explained. I just found out by myself. I think it was terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she, I think, was-- she had a miscarriage before. So she probably was sick, or maybe she wasn't well when she was bringing me up. Or maybe she was depressed. When I think back, the life that I have now and the lives of people lead here, how hard it was, the whole atmosphere in Poland, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never went back at all. I remember of an oppressive feeling, even as a child, maybe because I was sent away to the grandparents. And I remember this vividly. When the grandfather, I see him, how he came through the door. It was a big house, you know, first floor, and he appeared. And he said something. And there was something, screaming, and-- and going on. And I remember her mother hitting her--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother with you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Because we were always-- so I was with my mother's parents. My brother was with my father's parents. These were neighboring, you know, the town where my father lived. And then when I eventually came back and went to school and stayed till I run away, was in the middle, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Like, lets say you are Teaneck and to the left is Hackensack, to the right is Englewood. There might be 10, 12 kilometers distances. And there was a-- a bus that was going from one city to another. Let's say starting with the bigger city. And what is the-- the word? Was not cruising. What is for the bus? In the morning and in the evening there was a bus going. And people were going for business or something with the buses, just like here. But the distances were-- were bigger. How long did you stay with your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed three years. And why with my mother's parents. I don't think I saw my brother. I don't recall. But my father was coming every Sunday. Or was it every second Sunday? And he was talking to his, you know, in-laws, right? And he was visiting me and bringing me fairy tales, a little booklet, you know, little, you know, all this Christian, you know. What's his name? Anderson.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hans Christian Anderson.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hans Christian Anderson. I remember 'Little Mermaid' and all these books. And I was-- had a little-- little chair and a little table for me. And I was-- while he was visiting, he was coming and bringing me the cake, cookies, you know? Not cookie, they had this good, this Napoleons in Poland, very good bakeries. And I was eating this, and I was absorbed with my reading, you know, while he was there. By the time he left, I was already-- I finished. I loved this fairy tales.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, my father came. And he had the-- my grandmother was this-- this queen Sheba, you know? She was the one that my mother's, you know? She was-- probably have it from her, around. And she was sitting on a little higher chair. And he was talking with her. And my father was talking and something. And she was arguing. And he started to cry. And I resented it. I resented my father, you know? I somehow wanted my father to be a macho figure, right? But he was soft. He was-- and he was crying. I didn't understand what it was all about. Later, I realized that he probably announced them that he is getting married again. Was three years after. And they were against it. I don't know why, what it-- what this-- this-- I don't know milieu was that something that there might be in these Jewish circles that she didn't want him to remarry, you know? But he had two children, right? He needed somebody. He had-- he lived there in this Debica with his brother who helped him in business. And they had the housekeeper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was never told about it. I realized that this must have been. And he was just pleading. And so I know that he got-- he remarried. He married a young woman, 21 years old. And she must have been from a not well-to-do family because otherwise, she wouldn't have married a widower with two children. I am-- I mention this because this is how it was, these mores. There's such a provincial way of life, you know, in Poland, that mentioning about that she was poor or something, you know, I have to-- I must mention it here. People, the President are proud of their poor background, right, what the parents achieved. And in Poland was just the opposite. And then we know that there were so many geniuses among the poor people that never had a chance to-- to-- to develop because, you know. So anyhow, so I was there. I was going to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: You then moved--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you moved back?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, I was going to school in this little town, right, for three years, the third grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which town was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This Ropczyce where my, you know, know mother's parents lived, where I was stayed the three years after my mother died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Helena, would you spell Ropczyce?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-P-C-Z-Y-C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was there going to school, was a very good student. I had little girlfriends, walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this-- was this a public school or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school, elementary school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was just one-- one school for girls, and there was another school for boys somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many Jewish classmates that you had in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was was no-- I don't remember any distinctions at all. I always had everything, you know, there was no A's, B's. It was very good, good, you know? I was all very good, had these friends. And I didn't realize that, but they probably spoiled me, you know, the uncles, three uncles and then aunt. And, you know, they took care of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were your mother's brothers and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's brothers, yes, I was sick. Maybe I had-- I don't know-- rubella or something. I remember myself having a, you know, the normal childhood thing. And then came vacation in the summer. And my grandmother, you know, didn't allow me to, you know, to go to visit my father, you know? He was coming. One day, I was supposed to take this bus that was-- what is the right word? Not cruising, when the bus goes from one stop to another. Well, all right, you know, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --bus made many stops along the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Many stops, yes. And I was told that-- so I was already, I mean, independent. They were bringing me to the bus. The bus driver, they knew the bus driver. He knew the families. It was such a family thing. The bus driver, you know, they seated-- they paid my ticket. They seated next to the bus driver. I didn't complain. And I was to going by myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other second town or third town, the other grandparent, somebody waited for me to pick me up. I was told not to step in Debica, right? I have to go through and go to [INAUDIBLE] where my father is. That's what I was going on vacation. So, I mean, isn't important-- is it important to talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to with the bus. And then in Debica, door opens and I see some strange people and somebody. And they ask the driver. They talk to the driver. And they took me, you know, almost like kidnapped me. And that's how I came back to my-- my father. And I met my stepmother. So this was her-- her brother that took me. So I just wasn't allowed to continue. They took me off this bus. And that's how I returned to my father and my brother. And they were much better off now. There was a maid. And I must have been, you know, nine years old. I remember she tried to-- to dress me, and I pushed her away because I was, you know, independent. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were those happy days for you being back with your father and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm, not exactly. I was-- I felt I was somehow became an outsider. My stepmother, you know, it wasn't easy. I never wanted to call her mother. I called her aunt, auntie. And with my brother, I didn't see him. There was never this close relationship. And he had a different upbringing. He was-- they were sending him, like everybody, you know, to a-- in the afternoons, he was going-- he was going to public school. And he was going to a boys school. I was going to the girls school. And he was getting the Hebrew education, you know, studying the Talmud and the-- somehow, he was a loner. He was-- he looked different, and he was slim and tall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember that when I came to, you know, fourth grade, right, this was a bigger city. There were many children in the classes. I wasn't known, right? I was sitting in the back. And I guess this was very important to me because I wasn't called to answer. And when I got my first grades, I had C's and things. And it was heartbreaking. It was so terrible. It was my first big, big-- Disappointment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Disappointment, yes. And I was sitting, you know, and my think my head and crying. But then saw my stepmother. I never realized how good she was. They took a tutor for me, started. But then I didn't need a tutor. So the second, the end of the year, I already had everything, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make friends in your new home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I made friends. I had Polish friends. And I was very good in school. I was praised for my Polish, for my writing. And then I finished the-- how much do you want me to talk about this? My stepmother, who was, you know, a very good housekeeper, then she had two children of her own. I loved these children. I was babysitting, taking them for-- for walks. And we were going on vacations to the mountains. But I had no communication with her like with a mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll take a break now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
